15.1.label: Question 15.1
15.1.question:
Suggest two possible problems that might result from using fixed annual percentage increases to
IP benefits.

Solution: If the inflation of living costs is higher than the fixed rate, policyholders may become under‐
insured. Policyholders may be very disappointed if their policies fail to cover the costs they were
originally designed for, which may lead to bad public relations and significant marketing risk,
eg producing reduced future sales and/or higher lapse rates.
If the inflation of living costs is lower than the fixed rate, policyholders may become over‐insured.
If premiums are also increasing at the same fixed rate, this may also make premiums too
expensive, thus increasing lapse rates. Over‐insurance is a particular problem if it happens to IP
policies, where benefits may be capped at the time of claiming to keep benefits within the
maximum permissible replacement ratio. Policyholders caught in this way could be seriously
dissatisfied, producing knock‐on adverse marketing effects.
Overall the problems arise through the policy not meeting customer needs properly.

15.2.label: Question 15.2
15.2.scenario:
For PMI, the policies in force in any one year are a mixture of policies renewed from the previous
year, and policies issued to individuals for the first time. However, the same premium is usually
charged in either case.
15.2.question: Suggest and explain how you would allow for the relevant expenses when pricing the product.

Solution: Initial and renewal expenses – Initial expenses are likely to be significantly greater than those
associated with renewals (eg initial medical underwriting). An assumption is usually made about
the number of years new policies are expected to be renewed, and the excess of the initial costs
over the renewal costs are spread over this number of years. Adding this annual (amortised)
initial expense to the expected renewal cost for the year gives the total per‐policy administration
expense assumption.
Commission would be dealt with in a similar way, as initial commission rates are set at a higher
level than renewal commission (in order to attract new business), but would normally be
expressed as a percentage of premium.
Claims administration expenses – These would typically be allowed for by a percentage increase
to the average claim cost assumed for the year.

15.3.label: Question 15.3
15.3.scenario:
Explain how future developments in medicine might be expected to affect claim costs under the
   following health and care insurance contracts:

CI insurance: Medical developments can increase the rate of diagnosis of illnesses.                                [½]
This can make claims occur sooner, ...                                                               [½]
... or it can make claims occur during the policy term, which would previously have occurred only
after the end of the contract (with no cost to the insurer).                                   [½]
Both cases will increase the cost of claims, especially the second case.                             [½]
Some illnesses will be diagnosed that would never have otherwise been recognised.                    [½]
Medical advances may prevent some illnesses from occurring, thereby reducing claim costs.            [½]
Some critical illness claims relate to the need for specific expensive treatments (such as heart
Medical advances can mean that some of these treatments no longer occur, so reducing claim
Alternatively, medical advances can enable many more people to receive the treatment, thereby

IP insurance: The effect depends on the type of illness or disability involved with the claim.                           [½]
If the condition is chronic, so that the claimant is unlikely ever to return to work, medical
advances may prolong life and hence increase the duration over which the claim payments are
If the condition is only temporary, then medical advances should hasten recovery and so reduce
In some cases, recovery may now occur during the deferred period, thus eliminating these claim
Medical advances that reduce the incidence of disease will reduce claim costs.                             [½]

PMI: If medical advances make more expensive treatments available, then this will increase claim costs.
Previously expensive treatments may become more routine and so less expensive, thus reducing
Treatments may become more effective, reducing the duration of treatment and the associated
costs, such as the cost of hospital stays.                                                [½]
Some advances may remove the need for treatment altogether, again reducing claim costs. [½]

15.4.label: Question 15.4
15.4.scenario:
A health and care insurance company is setting the pricing bases for some of its products.

   Discuss the importance of the assumptions made about the investment return, the expenses and
the withdrawal rates, when setting the pricing bases for the following products:

Investment return: The reserves that build up under this product are comparatively small, so the price is relatively
insensitive to this assumption. Hence it won’t be too serious if the assumption turns out to be
wrong.                                                                                            [½]
Therefore the investment return is not a key assumption.                                                   [½]
Expenses: Competition for this business is often on price, therefore there will be fairly small margins in the
premium rates.                                                                                     [½]
This is the second most important assumption for this class of contract after morbidity. The
typically small premium size makes the expenses more significant.                                          [½]
As the market is likely to be price‐sensitive, the expense assumption is likely to be critical in
determining the volume of sales and hence in maximising the total contribution to overheads and
profit.                                                                                           [½]
However, if the assumption used is too low, the insurer may write far more new business than
expected, but at a loss; ...                                                                 [½]
... if the assumption used is too high, new business volumes may be much lower than expected,
with the result that contributions towards fixed costs are insufficient.                     [½]
Withdrawal rates: There is usually no benefit payable on withdrawal, but there will still be lapses.                       [½]
These are likely to be from among the healthier lives, and so the expected future morbidity
experience of the remaining portfolio will be heavier.                                                  [½]
The insurer will make a loss on a particular policy if it lapses when its asset share is negative,
otherwise there will be a profit.                                                                        [½]
So, the importance of this assumption is different for early and late withdrawals.                 [½]
[Maximum 4]

(ii).question: A without‐profits immediate needs LTC annuity.

Investment return: Investment return, mortality and (in those cases where the annuity payments vary with the
degree of incapacity) morbidity, are the key assumptions for this product.                              [½]
The single premium, less initial expenses, must be invested at outset to provide a future,
guaranteed income stream. The amount of this income stream will depend crucially on the
investment returns available at the outset.                                                             [½]
Assuming that the benefits are inflation‐linked, the single premium is likely to be invested in
index‐linked bonds of appropriate duration (if such bonds exist).                                       [½]
A highly matched strategy should secure a relatively certain real yield. If such a strategy is used, it
is essential that the annuity pricing assumption is not higher than this yield, as this would lock the
insurer into a guaranteed loss.                                                                     [½]
The pricing assumption will need to be reduced in any case – both as a result of the uncertainty of
the liability duration due to variable mortality, and to the extent that appropriate matching assets
may be unavailable.                                                                              [½]
This assumption must be reviewed regularly in the light of changing investment conditions.              [½]
Expenses: The expense assumptions are important because terms are guaranteed, ...                                 [½]
... but less so than investment return as the relative impact of expenses on profit is lower.            [½]
The majority of expenses will occur at the outset (eg underwriting, contract issue) and their
amounts will be better known.                                                                               [½]
Withdrawal rates: There is very unlikely to be any withdrawal benefit and no further premiums are payable, and so
there is no reason for anyone to withdraw from this product.                                  [½]
Therefore this assumption is not at all important.                                                          [½]
However, if there were a withdrawal benefit, the assumption could become very important,
depending on the size of the withdrawal benefit payable.                                 [½]
[Maximum 4]

(iii).question:
A large unit‐linked group IP insurance. A monthly charge is deducted to pay for the cost
of sickness claims commencing during the month. A level percentage of the unit fund is
also deducted each month to cover expenses. The employer pays all contributions and is
entitled to the residual unit fund value on discontinuance of the scheme. (In this context,
withdrawal refers to the ending of the whole group scheme by an employer, rather than
to individual members leaving the scheme.)

Investment return: The investment risk is largely passed to the policyholder (employer) and there are no investment
guarantees.                                                                                   [½]
Therefore, from this perspective, this is not a key assumption.                                             [½]
However, the only charge received by the insurer to cover expenses is a percentage of fund, ...
... which makes the unit growth rate very important.                                                        [½]
The employer’s contribution rate will reflect the investment return assumption used. It is
important to manage expectations and explain the risks and the uncertainty involved.                        [½]
The appropriateness of a prudent or optimistic assumption for this purpose will depend on the
employer’s attitude to risk and willingness to increase premiums later, if necessary.         [½]
Expenses: The importance of this assumption depends on whether the insurer can vary the monthly
charge.                                                                                                     [½]
If ‘no’, then the assumption is very important.                                                             [½]
If ‘yes’, then not so important, but the insurer will need to consider expectations of its
customers.                                                                                                  [½]
Also, there would still be a delay before the charge increase could be implemented, and so there
would be a continuing loss in the meantime.                                                    [½]
Withdrawal rates: The insurer should consider how the discontinuance benefit (the value of the unit fund) compares
with the asset share.                                                                        [½]
An important factor is the incidence of charges relative to expenses. The charges will start off
very low (fund starts at zero) and slowly increase over time, although the level may well be
volatile.                                                                                        [½]
In contrast, the initial expenses will be substantial, with ongoing expenses much lower, but
steadily increasing.                                                                                    [½]
This will mean that the unit fund value will be much in excess of asset share at early durations; at
later durations the asset share will catch up with, and ultimately overtake, the unit fund.       [½]
Therefore, there will be a large loss on early discontinuance, as no early termination penalty
applies.                                                                                               [½]
So, in the absence of such a penalty, the withdrawal assumption will be very important.     [½]
[Maximum 6]

15.5.label: Question 15.5
15.5.scenario:
A health and care insurer issues a without‐profits long‐term care insurance product, paid for by

single premiums. The policy guarantees to pay 75% of the policyholder’s long‐term care costs as
they arise, subject to appropriate medical certification of need. Only those in good health are
allowed to take out a policy. Surrender is not permitted.

You are required to derive the assumptions needed, in order to value just the benefit part of the
insurer’s supervisory reserve for this product.
15.5.question:
Describe how these assumptions would be determined, including the experience investigations
that would be carried out.

For each assumption:          work out the best estimate projection of future experience                                   [½]
         put in margins so that the basis will be suitably prudent (if regulation requires prudent
         ensure consistency between assumptions, eg investment returns and inflation                  [½]
         ensure that any conditions required by regulations are met.                                  [½]
Transition rates and mortality rates: The insurer would analyse its own experience data, as far as possible.                                 [½]
It would compare actual against expected numbers of transitions, probably using last year’s
valuation assumptions as the expected basis.                                                            [½]
Alternatively, it would compare its own experience with a standard table, though this may not be
available for all types of transition.                                                        [½]
Recent data should be used.                                                                            [½]
It will probably be necessary to use several years’ data for credibility, as the data are likely to be
sparse, ...                                                                                          [½]
... but there may be problems of heterogeneity if the period of the investigation is too long.         [½]
It should calculate exposed to risk for each care state separately.                                    [½]
It can then multiply by appropriate expected transition rates (according also to age, gender etc) to
get expected numbers.                                                                            [½]
The insurer would analyse by risk group (gender, smoker etc) if possible, but this is unlikely to be
very reliable unless the portfolio is very large.                                                  [½]
It should also try to identify any trends in the experience over time. This investigation could be
used to decide on a suitable projection formula for each transition rate.                          [½]
Industry and reinsurers’ data would be looked at, if available, to get more credible observations.
These should provide more reliable information on trends, in particular.                                     [½]
However, these data are less relevant due to different policy types involved, different benefit
conditions etc.                                                                                 [½]
It may be useful to look at population data (eg nursing home statistics).                                    [½]
Judgement would be made from the data about the expected future experience.                                  [½]
An allowance should be made for any trends.                                                                  [½]
A prudent valuation might only allow for adverse trends.                                                     [½]
Any known or expected changes that might affect future transition rates would be allowed for. [½]
For example, there may be changes to underwriting / selling practices / policy conditions.                   [½]
Where prudence is required, the basis would need to include substantial margins for adverse
experience, because the future experience is so uncertain.                                  [½]
Prudence could be included in the basis:          using lower (lighter) than best estimate mortality assumptions                                     [½]
         using higher than best estimate rates for transitions from healthy to sick states, and for
transitions from one sick state to a higher (more expensive) sick state.                   [½]
It is possible that last year’s valuation assumptions will be used again if there is little evidence to
suggest that they should be changed.                                                                  [½]
This is to avoid creating unnecessary valuation profits or losses due to arbitrary basis changes
Benefit amounts and benefit inflation: The insurer’s own claims amount experience data for a recent period would be used as a starting
point, for identifying the average annual benefit amount per claimant.                        [½]
Analysis by duration of claim would be useful, as costs would be expected to increase with time as
health deteriorates.                                                                           [½]
Analysing by location may be useful in identifying any geographical differences in costs.                    [½]
Analysis by age, gender and smoker status may also reveal differences (although such differences
are probably less important).                                                                 [½]
Any industry data for this type of product would be looked at. Adjustments may need to be made
to allow for differences, such as in location and the extent of coinsurance under other insurers’
policies (compared with the 25% coinsurance under this particular policy).                        [1]
Relevant nursing home, residential home and domiciliary care data (if available) would be
analysed.                                                                                              [½]
Adjustments would be made for past inflation to bring the average benefit amounts up to levels
current at the valuation date.                                                               [½]
Longer historical periods of all data would be analysed to see how past benefit inflation rates
might be correlated with other rates (eg national price or earnings inflation).                 [½]
The insurer would extrapolate current inflation rates into the future …                                [½]
… taking the general economic outlook into account, including expected prospects for other
related inflation rates.                                                                                [½]
Factors that might affect long‐term care costs differently would be taken into account, for
example:
         increasing demand due to an ageing population could increase care costs                      [½]
         future legislation (eg on standards of care) may affect the prices that the care institutions
can charge.                                                                                [½]
Reinsurers may be able to give useful advice.                                                          [½]
Prudence could be included by using higher benefit amounts and higher inflation rates.                  [½]
Interest rates: The insurer should consider the asset mix backing the in‐force business.                               [½]
This would differ depending on the policyholder’s current state (ie whether healthy, or receiving
The future experience assumption would be based on the expected future returns on these
These should be the expected returns relative to the supervisory values of the assets, allowing for
the method (and assumptions) used for the asset valuation.                                       [½]
There will be reinvestment of future investment income for those policies not yet claiming. So
the insurer must also consider expected returns from this, and combine with yield expected from
current assets to get an overall investment return assumption.                                 [1]
There will be much less reinvestment for claims in payment, unless assets and liabilities are
Any margin for prudence will depend on the types of assets used (though the insurer will probably
have chosen index‐linked government bonds as much as possible, to match the inflation‐linked
A margin could be used to cover:          mismatching, eg by duration, type of index link                                                [½]
Prudence could be included in the basis by reducing the interest rate from best estimate
Alternatively, if a market‐consistent approach is used then all cashflows would be discounted at

Syllabus objective: 5.1     Describe the principles of setting assumptions for health and care insurance business.
5.1.1      Describe the principles of setting assumptions for pricing health and care
5.1.2      Describe the principles of setting assumptions for determining liabilities.
5.1.3      Explain why the assumptions used for supervisory reserves may be different
5.1.4      Outline the principles of setting assumptions for determining embedded
This chapter covers some final assumptio: It starts by describing new business assumptions – in particular new business volumes and new
business mix. Note that these are assumptions that will be needed primarily for pricing, but not
for many other purposes, because for most other purposes (eg reserving and assessing
profitability) actual new business volumes and mix will be known.
It also looks at the determination of the risk discount rate. As in previous chapters, the focus of
this chapter is pricing, although there will be similar considerations for other purposes.
1 New business volume: A further element in the actuary’s assumptions is the likely future volume of new business
to be written of each policy type.
This will assist in the allocation of expenses to the premium basis for each typical policy.
The effect on the expense assumption has already been discussed (in Chapter 15).
It will also be important in assessing the amount of company reserves that will be needed to
The point about reserves is that the insurer needs to be sure that it has enough capital available
to meet the new business strain (to a great extent caused by the supervisory reserving
requirement). The total amount of capital required will be a function of the volume of new
2 New business mix: This section first considers the main risk associated with incorrectly predicting the mix of new
2.1 Mix of business risk: In an ideal world, the premium charged for each individual policy would be sufficient to meet the
expected cost of claims and expenses for that policy. However, in practice, it may be necessary to
make ‘average’ assumptions about groups of policies, and so to have a degree of cross‐subsidy
between policies within each group. In this case, it will therefore be necessary to make
assumptions about the mix of new business.
It is worth stressing that with any of the above averaging and cross-subsidies, the insurer
may be laying itself open to anti-selection if it attracts a higher proportion of the higher risk
lives than expected in the pricing basis.
This may well happen if a competitor prices with more detailed subdivision and hence wins
more of the lighter risks. Actuaries must monitor the experience in terms of the categories
of risk cells becoming new business, in order to reappraise their pricing assumptions on a
So the problem is that because the insurer has had to charge average premiums to a
heterogeneous group of lives, it will only receive the necessary amount of premium in total if the
assumed mix of business (for the group concerned) materialises in practice. Because consumers
are always seeking the best value for money, the tendency is always for a greater than expected
proportion of the higher‐risk lives to take out insurance, so causing the anti‐selection described in
Risks are covered in detail in Chapters 23‐25. (More specifically, new business risk is described in
2.2 Average policy size: An estimation of policy size (eg sum insured) will be a factor in the allocation of expenses.
The implications of this for expected claims experience and expenses was discussed in Chapter 15.
The insurer therefore needs to make an assumption for the expected average size of policy in the
pricing model. This would be based on the insurer’s past experience for the product (if any) and
market data relating to similar products, allowing for any trends that might be expected to
The average policy size may be linked to the socio-economic profile of the target market.
In addition, considerations of inflation and general levels of consumer demand for the product are
relevant in trying to estimate this parameter.
2.3 Split by product: The actuary will need an estimate of the company’s future business for each product line so
that indirect expenses may be charged accurately to each contract for pricing purposes.
Future product numbers and sizes may also be required for the allocation of solvency
2.4 Split by distribution channel: Where healthcare insurance products are priced uniformly but sold through different
distribution channels, the actuary will need projections of the volumes (and related
expenses) to be sold through each channel. These assumptions will need to be monitored,
especially if actual rates of commission paid differ from the commission levels assumed in
2.5 Split by territory: As already seen, the health and care risk can vary substantially between territories and even
significantly between different regions within one country. For example, income protection
insurance risk can vary dramatically between a heavy industrial urban centre and an
The actuary may use the region in which the policyholder lives as a rating factor for
premiums. If region is not used as a rating factor, the actuary will have to allow for
differences in health and care risk through averages in the product costing.
For any cross-subsidies of this nature, forecasts of business volumes by territory will be
2.6 Split by gender: In some territories and for some lines, the market practice (or legislation) dictates that an
insurer does not discriminate between the sexes in pricing healthcare.
The morbidity risk will almost certainly vary by gender and thus the actuary will need
estimates of relative numbers and sizes so that accurate ‘unisex’ prices might be charged.
As is the case in the sections above, it will be necessary to forecast the business volumes by
2.7 Split by occupation: As we have already discussed, the occupation of the policyholders have a material bearing
on their propensity to make a health and care insurance claim.
Where occupation is not a risk factor or where there are broad groupings of trades, the
actuary will have to allow for likely volumes at each risk level, so that an adequate premium
can be charged over the whole portfolio.
3.1 Introduction: This section derives a suitable risk discount rate to use in pricing models.
It is reasonable to suppose that the owners of an insurance company decide where to invest
by comparing the returns offered by different companies, relative to the risks that are run,
and can move their capital from one company to another if they wish.
An investor will demand a higher expected rate of return from a risky investment than from
a safe investment to compensate the investor for the risks of default, commercial failure and
so on. This is often expressed by saying that the investor requires a ‘risk premium’ over
and above the expected returns on safer investments.
It is useful to borrow from economic theory the concept of the ‘risk-free’ asset, namely an
asset that offers a certain return, absolutely free from all risk of default.
Risk‐free assets are sometimes taken to be government bonds in certain, stable countries.
Investing in an insurance company is certainly not the same as investing in the economist’s
idealised risk-free asset. Investors will demand an expected rate of return equal to the
risk-free rate plus a risk premium. Such a rate of return is called a ‘risk discount rate’. The
question is, what risk premium is appropriate to compensate for the risks of investing in an
Recall that there are likely to be two k:          the return required by the insurance company, (or in this context, the shareholders in
         the level of statistical risk attaching to the cashflows under the particular contract (or
project); this may be thought of as an addition (or reduction) on the shareholders’
required return resulting from specific projects in which the insurance company is
The first aspect is often assessed using the Capital Asset Pricing Model. In the case of the ‘project’
being the pricing of a new product, the second aspect may be thought of as a ‘product‐specific’
3.2 The Capital Asset Pricing Model: Recall that the question is ‘what is an appropriate risk premium for shareholders investing in the
The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) has been widely used by stock market investors to
answer exactly this sort of question. The idea behind CAPM is that a well-diversified
portfolio of shares cancels out the risks of investing in individual shares and leaves only
the unavoidable risks of investing in the stock exchange.
A suitable proxy can be chosen to represent risk-free assets, such as short-term deposits
issued by a stable government, and the average risk premium that the diversified portfolio
of shares has yielded over the risk-free rate, over a period of time, is estimated.
The short‐term deposits here are just one example of an asset that is (almost) risk‐free.
Alternatives are very short‐dated government bonds, or long‐dated index‐linked government
bonds. In practice, it will be impossible to find any assets that are perfectly risk‐free.
So, the aim is to quantify the risk of investing in this company’s shares (compared to the risk‐free
asset). This risk may be measured as the difference in return between:
         a well‐diversified portfolio of shares, eg represented by a suitable index
 some risk‐free asset, eg a sho: The returns from these two assets may be compared over a period of time to iron out any random
The question can then be asked as to how risky a particular company’s shares are
compared with the diversified portfolio. The result of the CAPM is that the proper risk
premium for any particular share is in proportion to its beta.
E i  r   Em  r   i: where:              Ei  E ri  is the expected return on asset i (eg our company’s shares)
r is the return on a risk‐free asset
Em  E rm  is the expected market retu: i is the ‘beta factor’ of asset i (defined as the covariance of ri and rm divided by
The beta factor i can be thought of as a measure of the riskiness of the asset relative to that of
the market. A value greater than 1 implies that, when the market is rising, the asset’s value will
increase more than the market average; and conversely, when the market is falling, it will
decrease in value more than the market average.
This process identifies the systematic (or market) risk of the asset, ie the risk that cannot be
eliminated by diversification or making the same investment many times over.
The CAPM is just one example of how the market might assess the shares of a company.
The point to note is that it is not up to the actuary alone to decide what an appropriate rate
of return is for shareholders. The actuary will need to make some assumptions, but the
3.3 Allowing for the product‐specific: So, CAPM can give the value of Ei : the rate of return that shareholders expect from investing in
this company to compensate for the risk involved. However this overall rate cannot be used as
the risk discount rate in the pricing models because not all projects are equally risky.
The insurer should view itself as an investor like any other when it considers the riskiness
of a new product, as in the long run the profits emerging from the whole company are the
profits emerging from the products that it sells. A change in the mix of business, for
example away from old and ‘safe’ contracts towards new and innovative contracts, would
change the market’s evaluation of the company’s riskiness. In practice, competition can
make it very difficult to allow properly for the very high-risk business.
So if a company proposes to launch a new product with innovative design features, this will
change the overall risk premium demanded by the company’s shareholders, and the only way to
satisfy this demand is for this product to generate a higher return on capital. In other words, the
risk discount rate used needs to be higher, and basically the higher the risk, the higher the risk
discount rate the company should use.
The following are among the features that can make a product design riskier, viewed as an
Question: What features of a critical illness product might lead to a lower risk discount rate?
 there is a lot of reliable dat:          no (or not very onerous) guarantees, eg reviewable premium rates
         no policyholder options, eg to renew or increase the sum insured
         low overhead costs (since this reduces the risk posed by low business levels)
 simple benefits / definitions,:          a well‐known market, eg where CI insurance is an established product.
It is not easy to assess these risks, and it is even harder to say what effect they should have
Each of these risks will increase the uncertainty of the cashflows in our pricing model. The
variability of the cashflow components is sometimes referred to as statistical risk.
The level of statistical risk could be a:          in some situations, analytically, by considering the variances of the individual
If we can estimate distributions for certain parameter values, then it may be possible to
derive the variance of that variable, which can be used to give an indication of the
         by using sensitivity analysis, as described in Chapter 11, with deterministically
assessed variations in the parameter values
Sensitivity analysis shows to what changes in the assumptions the profits are most
susceptible. If this was, say, an increase in the assumption, the idea is then to find the
increased parameter value that would be assessed as corresponding to a 5% probability
level. (Note that this is a subjective assessment.)
By using this increased parameter value in the profit test, it is possible to calculate a
revised return on capital from the product (keeping all other items, including the
premium, unaltered). The change in the return obtained will (crudely) show how the
variance of an individual parameter translates into the variance of return on capital.
         by using stochastic models for some, or all, of the parameter values and simulation
Here, the important parameter values would be varied stochastically according to their
assumed probability distribution, re‐computing the rate of return for each new scenario.
Doing this, say, 1,000 times will then give a good idea of the variance of the rate of return.
3.4 Consolidating the two approaches: Finally it is necessary to consider how the two approaches – that of using the shareholders
required return (eg using CAPM) and the statistical approach described above – can be brought
together.
If the overall required return Ei is considered to be a true reflection of the riskiness of investing in
the company as a whole, then projects that are less risky than average will require a lower risk
discount rate than Ei , while projects that are more risky than average will require a higher risk
discount rate than Ei . This relative riskiness can be assessed using the statistical approaches
described above.
In reality, the assessment of the risk, and the conversion of that into a risk discount rate, is a very
inexact science. The most important points to remember are that:
         The risk discount rate must be higher than the risk‐free rate, hence changes in market
rates of interest should cause risk discount rates to change.
         The margin between the risk‐free rate and the risk discount rate should attempt to reflect
all sources of risk in the product and the risk discount rates used for pricing different
products should reflect the relative risk of those products.
         The overall return earned on the whole capital of the company, generated from its entire
business activities, needs to meet the required rate of return (cost of capital).
         The risk discount rate is simply a number that is used as a criterion in profit testing. It is
set to ensure that the rate of return from the product is satisfactory, given the inherent
variability of the return and the required return on capital.
3.5 Market consistency: So far, the approach has been to set the discount rate using CAPM (Section 3.2) and then allow for
the product‐specific risk for the contract being written (Section 3.3).
An alternative approach is to perform a market-consistent valuation of the cashflows. The
‘risk neutral’ approach to such a valuation effectively involves the use of risk-free interest
rates for the discount rates, which are generally modelled as being term dependent.
Swap rates or government bond yield curves are often used to set risk‐free term dependent
It is then likely that some form of margin would be included in respect of other parameters
used in the pricing, such as morbidity and expenses, to allow for the inherent risks in their
estimation (see also Chapter 13). This risk margin would reflect the compensation required
by the ‘market’ in return for taking on those uncertain aspects of the liability cashflows.
Even when not used as the primary methodology, a market-consistent approach should be
a useful tool in ensuring that the results from traditional approaches are reasonable and for
ensuring that any risk margin is justified.

cover fixed expenses, and to judge adequacy of capital to support new sales.

 by comparison with available m: Alternatively, instead of including risk margins in the risk discount rate, margins could be
Market consistency: Market‐consistent (or ‘risk neutral’) valuations can be carried out using risk‐free interest
rates for discount rates. Margins are then included in other parameter values to allow for
the inherent risks in their estimation. It is also useful as a reasonableness check on
traditional approaches and ensuring that risk margins are appropriate.
